locked_facts: AC generator converts mechanical energy to electrical energy using electromagnetic induction
EMF: ε = NBAω sin(ωt) = ε₀ sin(ωt)
Peak EMF: ε₀ = NBAω (N = turns, B = field, A = area, ω = angular speed)
When coil plane is parallel to B (θ = 90°): maximum rate of flux change → maximum EMF
When coil plane is perpendicular to B (θ = 0°): maximum flux but zero rate of change → zero EMF
Slip rings maintain continuous electrical contact as coil rotates
Frequency of AC = rotational frequency of coil: f = ω/(2π)
truth_statements: An AC generator produces alternating EMF that varies sinusoidally with time
The EMF is maximum when the coil plane is parallel to the magnetic field — this is when flux is changing most rapidly
The EMF is zero when the coil plane is perpendicular to B — at this instant the flux is maximum but not changing
Increasing the angular speed increases both the peak EMF and the frequency of the output
The key components are a coil, permanent magnets, slip rings, and brushes

aha_moment.what_to_show: Split screen: LEFT shows coil rotating in magnetic field with flux arrows. RIGHT shows ε vs t sinusoidal graph building in real time. When coil is parallel to B (flux changing fastest) → EMF peaks. When coil is perpendicular to B (max flux but not changing) → EMF = 0.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Coil orientation synced with the sine wave — EMF peaks when flux changes fastest, not when flux is maximum
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students confuse maximum flux with maximum EMF — seeing EMF peak when coil is parallel to B (not perpendicular) is the breakthrough

simulation_states.label: Generator structure
simulation_states.description: A rectangular coil between magnetic poles. Slip rings and brushes shown. The coil is ready to rotate. Components labelled.
simulation_states.emphasis: Key parts: coil in magnetic field + slip rings for continuous contact

simulation_states.label: Coil rotates
simulation_states.description: Coil starts rotating. Flux through coil changes sinusoidally. EMF = -dΦ/dt follows. Graph of ε vs t builds as coil turns.
simulation_states.emphasis: As the coil rotates, the flux changes → EMF is induced

simulation_states.label: Phase relationship
simulation_states.description: When coil ⊥ B: Φ = max but dΦ/dt = 0 → ε = 0. When coil ∥ B: Φ = 0 but dΦ/dt = max → ε = max. EMF leads flux by 90°.
simulation_states.emphasis: Maximum EMF when flux is CHANGING fastest — NOT when flux is maximum
labels_visible: ε max when Φ changing fastest

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student controls rotation speed. EMF amplitude and frequency both change. Adjust N, B, A to see ε₀ = NBAω change. Sine wave updates in real time.
simulation_states.emphasis: Faster rotation = higher voltage AND higher frequency. More turns = more voltage.

common_misconceptions.belief: EMF is maximum when flux through the coil is maximum
common_misconceptions.correction: EMF depends on RATE OF CHANGE of flux (dΦ/dt), not flux itself. When Φ is at its maximum, dΦ/dt = 0 → ε = 0. EMF is maximum when Φ is changing fastest (coil parallel to B).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Sync coil position with EMF graph — EMF peaks at 90° when coil is parallel to B, not at 0° when coil is perpendicular

common_misconceptions.belief: DC and AC generators work on different principles
common_misconceptions.correction: Both work on the same Faraday's law principle. The difference is in the output connections: AC uses slip rings (continuous output), DC uses a split-ring commutator (rectifies the output to pulsating DC).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show same coil with slip rings (AC output) vs commutator (rectified DC output)
